answers.field_label: Special Needs Or Medical Conditions We Should Be Aware Of
answers.value:
I don’t have any major medical conditions that would prevent me from fully participating in the conference, but there are a few minor things that may be helpful for the organizers to know in case of an emergency or when planning seating and room arrangements.

1) Mild seasonal allergies: I have mild seasonal allergies (primarily to grass and tree pollen). This usually just means I may sneeze a bit more if outdoor sessions or networking events are held in grassy areas, but it does not require any special medical support. I usually carry over-the-counter antihistamines (cetirizine) with me and take them as needed.

2) Sensitivity to very strong fragrances: Strong artificial fragrances (especially some perfumes and heavily scented cleaning products) can occasionally trigger mild headaches or light nausea for me. It’s not severe, but if possible I prefer to sit a bit away from heavily scented candles or diffusers. If there is a designated fragrance-free or low-scent area in conference rooms, I’d appreciate being able to sit there.

3) Food-related note (not an allergy): I do not have any food allergies, but I try to avoid large amounts of dairy during the daytime sessions because it tends to make me a bit sluggish and can occasionally upset my stomach. I’m fine with small amounts of cheese or milk in coffee, but I generally prefer non-dairy milk options (oat or almond) if they are available. This is more of a preference than a medical requirement, but it does help me stay more alert and engaged during long sessions.

4) General physical needs: I am able to walk and stand without assistance, but I do have a minor lower back issue from an old sports injury (nothing serious). If sessions involve prolonged standing, I may look for a chair or a place to sit occasionally. During longer plenary sessions, I really appreciate chairs with at least minimal back support. No special accommodations are strictly necessary, but I mention this in case there is any flexibility around seating or stretch breaks.

Overall, I’m fully able to participate in all activities, including the tours and regular conference schedule. These notes are just to give context in case there are options for seating areas, fragrance-light zones, or non-dairy beverage alternatives.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Anything Else We Should Know About You
answers.value:
I’m attending as a representative of Whitaker Analytics Group, a small data consulting firm I founded in 2018 that focuses on helping mid-sized nonprofits and membership associations make better use of their data for decision-making. My background is in economics and statistics, and I spent about ten years working inside a national nonprofit before moving into consulting. Because of that, I tend to approach sponsorships like this as both a networking opportunity and a chance to genuinely support the community I’m working with.

A few personal details that might be useful for planning and context:

1) Professional interests: I’m especially interested in sessions related to data governance, responsible AI use for associations, and practical case studies about improving member engagement through analytics. If there are any breakout groups or roundtables on those topics, I’d be very eager to join or even contribute informally. I’ve done several workshops on using dashboards to tell stories to boards of directors, and I’m always happy to share slide decks or examples if that would be helpful.

2) Personality and networking style: I’m fairly outgoing once I’ve met people, but I tend to be a little quiet during the first hour of an event while I get my bearings. If there are any structured networking activities (e.g., small-group introductions, topic tables, or sponsor meet-and-greet sessions), those really help me connect with people more naturally. I also make a point of being approachable at our sponsor table—if you’re assigning table locations, I don’t mind being in a fairly high-traffic area because I actually enjoy answering questions from attendees.

3) Travel details: I’ll be flying in from Austin on Monday afternoon and staying at the main conference hotel through Thursday morning. That means I’ll be available for Tuesday and Wednesday daytime and evening events, but I’ll likely head out early on Thursday to catch my flight. If there are any sponsor briefings or logistics meetings, it’s easiest for me to attend them on Tuesday morning before sessions begin or during a mid-morning break.

4) Dietary preferences: As mentioned above, I try to go light on dairy during the day and prefer balanced meals with some protein and vegetables so I’m not falling asleep after lunch. I’m not vegetarian or vegan, but I’m very happy with vegetarian options. If there are buffet-style meals, I usually build a plate with lean protein, vegetables, and something light like rice or salad. I drink coffee in the morning (ideally with oat milk if available) and switch to tea or water in the afternoon.

5) Hobbies and conversation starters: Outside of work, I’m a long-distance runner and usually get up early to run a few miles around the city I’m visiting. If there are safe running paths near the hotel, any maps or local tips would be appreciated. I also enjoy local coffee shops and bookstores, so recommendations from staff or other attendees are always welcome. I mention this because I’ve found that casual conversations about running routes or local spots often turn into really meaningful professional connections.

6) Accessibility and comfort: I don’t require formal accessibility accommodations, but I do appreciate clear signage and printed schedules or maps. I tend to take notes by hand as well as on my laptop, so a table surface or at least a chair with an armrest is helpful. If there are any quiet corners or lounge areas where people can step away from the noise for a few minutes, I often use those between sessions to recharge.

7) Sponsorship expectations: My main goals as a sponsor are (a) to learn more about the challenges associations are facing around data and technology, (b) to meet potential partners and clients in a low-pressure setting, and (c) to support this event so it continues in future years. I’m not interested in hard selling; I prefer longer-term relationships where we can be a helpful resource even if someone never becomes a paying client. If there are any guidelines about how sponsors should engage with attendees (e.g., limits on email follow-ups, expectations around prize drawings or swag), I’d appreciate having those clearly laid out so we can align with the culture of the event.

If you need any additional details from me ahead of time—such as a short bio, logo files, or information about any materials we plan to bring for our table—please feel free to reach out by email. I try to respond within 24 hours on weekdays.
